metadata.title: Bulla obliqua Handm. - Handmann 1883
metadata.description:
Bulla obliqua Handmann, 1883 [nomen nudum]

* Bulla obliqua Handm.— Handmann 1883: 64 [nomen nudum].

Discussion. Mentioned by Handmann (1883) in a faunal list from Gainfarn (Austria) without description.

metadata.title: Scaphander schollnbergeri Harzhauser, Landau & Malaquias, 2026, sp. nov.
metadata.description:
Scaphander schollnbergeri sp. nov.

Figs 58A 1 –A 2

? Scaphander lignarius grateloupi (Micht.) — Csepreghy-Meznerics 1972: 35, pl. 18, figs 16–17 [non Scaphander grateloupi (Michelotti, 1847) = Scaphander tarbelliana (Grateloup, 1837)].

Type material. Holotype, NHMW 1836/0012/6576a, SL: 14.3 mm, MD: 7.3 mm, Hrušovany nad Jevišovkou (Czech Republic), Figs 58A 1 –A 2.

Type locality. Hrušovany nad Jevišovkou (Czech Republic).

Type stratum. Middle Miocene silt and clay.

Age. Middle Miocene, middle Badenian (Langhian).

Etymology. In honor of Wolfgang E. Schollnberger (Potomac, MD, USA), in recognition of his contributions to Alpine geology.

Diagnosis. Small, moderately solid, ovate shell with sealed apical umbilicus, weak apical depression; sculpture of numerous, close-set spiral grooves separating spiral cords of equal width.

Description. Shell small, moderately solid, moderately broad, ovate (SL/MD = 1.9), moderately narrow posteriorly, slowly expanding abapically, not antero-dorsally compressed. Only last whorl visible. Apex narrow (MD/ApD = 2.9), slightly truncated, apical plane slightly oblique (AP = 10°), apical umbilicus fully sealed with shallowly concave depression. Periphery placed mid-whorl (SL/ PH = 2.0), weakly concave sides above periphery, forming angle of about 31°. Rounded at slightly flaring base. Sculpture on base of numerous, crowded spiral cords separated by punctate spiral grooves of similar width to cords. Punctations within grooves fused. Aperture as long as shell, moderately wide in adapical third (MD/AW = 6.0), widening greatly anteriorly (MD/MAW = 1.6), resulting in moderate expansion rate (ER = 3.7). Outer lip not rising above apex, expanding and almost straight below, broadly rounded abapically. Columella broadly excavated. Columellar callus forming moderate width, sharply delimited rim, narrowing adapically. No parietal callus.

Discussion. Unlike the small Scaphander species figured on Fig. 57 (hoc opus), this specimen is relatively thick shelled and the columellar callus is well developed, which suggests it represents an almost/fully adult shell. Scaphander schollnbergeri sp. nov. is characterized by its ovate outline, fully sealed umbilicus and dense spiral sculpture, which strongly weakens adapically. It is also unusual in that it is not dorso-ventrally compressed. Scaphander parvulinus Sacco, 1897, from the Tortonian and Pliocene of the Po Basin (Italy), might be a related species of comparable size (SL: 12 mm), which differs in its wider adapical aperture, wider maximum aperture width and larger expansion rate (see Sacco 1897: pl. 3, figs 100–101). Two specimens from Borsodbóta (Hungary) illustrated by Csepreghy-Meznerics (1972: pl. 18, figs 16–17) might be conspecific with Scaphander schollnbergeri sp. nov. but differ in their slightly more elongate outline and more prominent spiral sculpture. Their ovate outline and only weakly expanding base exclude a relation with Scaphander grateloupi (Michelotti, 1847) [= Scaphander tarbelliana (Grateloup, 1837)] as suggested by Csepreghy-Meznerics (1972).

Paleoenvironment. Unknown.

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): North Alpine-Carpathian Foreland Basin: Hrušovany nad Jevišovkou (Czech Republic) (hoc opus);? Bükk Mountains: Borsodbóta (Hungary) (Csepreghy-Meznerics 1972).

metadata.title: Scaphander catena Harzhauser, Landau & Malaquias, 2026, sp. nov.
metadata.description:
Scaphander catena sp. nov.

Figs 57D 1 –D 3

? Scaphander (Scaphander) lignarius lignarius (Linné, 1766) —Atanacković 1963: 81, pl. 15, fig. 9 [non Scaphander lignarius (Linnaeus, 1758)].

? Scaphander (Scaphander) lignarius lignarius (Linné, 1766) — Atanacković 1985: 187, pl. 42, fig. 3 [non Scaphander lignarius (Linnaeus, 1758)].

Type material. Holotype, NHMW 1997z0178/1217, SL: 12.7 mm, MD: 3.3 mm, Lăpugiu de Sus (Romania), Figs 57D 1 –D 3.

Type locality. Lăpugiu de Sus (Romania), Făget Basin.

Type stratum. Silt and clay of the Dej Formation.

Age. Middle Miocene, middle Badenian (Langhian).

Etymology. Latin catena (= chain), referring to the sculpture in the spiral grooves; noun in apposition.

Diagnosis. Small, solid, moderately broad shell with prominent, deep, relatively close-spaced spiral grooves with chain-like punctuation.

Description. Shell small, medium thickness, moderately broad (SL/MD = 1.8), narrow posteriorly, expanding abapically, slightly antero-dorsally compressed. Only last whorl visible. Apex narrow (MD/ApD = 3.4), slightly truncated, apical plane slightly oblique (AP = 10°), apical umbilicus nearly sealed, reduced to semicircular slit, delimited by sharp-edged, trigonal crest. Periphery placed mid-whorl (SL/ PH = 1.8), weakly concave sides above periphery, forming angle of about 48°. Rounded at slightly flaring base.Sculpture of relatively close-spaced, irregular, flat spiral cords, separated by relatively broad, punctate spiral grooves; punctations within grooves subcircular with weak crests, in apposition, forming chain-like sculpture within grooves. Spiral cords close-set at apex, becoming slightly wider below periphery, occasionally weakly bifid. Aperture as long as shell, relatively wide in adapical third (MD/AW = 3.9), widening greatly anteriorly (MD/MAW = 1.4), resulting in relatively low expansion rate (ER = 2.7). Outer lip only slightly rising above apex, expanding and almost straight below, broadly rounded abapically. Columella broadly excavated. Columellar callus forming narrow rim, delimiting narrow umbilical slit. Parietal region covered by sediment.

Discussion. The specimen herein described as Scaphander catena sp. nov. was mixed with shells of Scaphander grundincola (De Gregorio, 1885) in the NHMW collection, but differs from its congeners in its smaller size, much weaker columellar callus and chain-like spiral sculpture in the deeper and broader spiral grooves. It is quite possible that at 12.7 mm height it is a juvenile specimen and the columellar callus has still to mature. However, its sculpture of irregular cords separated by grooves with chain like punctuations differs from all its congeners; at similar size the punctuations are much coarser. We therefore consider formal description warranted despite the likely subadult nature of the holotype. We are not aware of similar species in the Circum-Mediterranean Neogene.

Paleoenvironment. Unknown.

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene):? Southern Pannonian Basin: Miljevići (Bosnia and Herzegovina) (Atanacković 1985); Făget Basin: Lăpugiu de Sus (Romania) (hoc opus).

metadata.title: Scaphander helveticus Peyrot 1932
metadata.description:
Scaphander helveticus Peyrot, 1932

Figs 56D 1 –D 2

* Scaphander lignarius Linné mut. helvetica nov. mut.— Peyrot 1932: 204, nr. 1411, pl. 12, figs 25–26.

Scaphander lignarius Linné, 1758 —Harzhauser 2002: 127, pl. 11, fig. 17 [non Scaphander lignarius (Linnaeus, 1758)].

Type material. Lectotype (designated herein), specimen illustrated in Peyrot (1932: pl. 12, figs 25–26), Serravallian (Middle Miocene), Salles (France).

Illustrated material. NHMW 2000z0001/0053, SL: 46.0 mm, MD: 28.0 mm, Niederkreuzstetten (Austria) (illustrated in Harzhauser 2002: pl. 11, fig. 17), Figs 56D 1 –D 2.

Revised description. Shell very large, solid, broad (SL/MD = 1.6), narrow posteriorly, strongly expanding abapically, slightly antero-dorsally compressed. Only last whorl visible. Apex narrow (MD/ApD = 4.6), slightly truncated, apical plane strongly oblique (AP = 18°), apical umbilicus fully sealed, delimited by cord-like, narrowly rounded crest. Periphery placed mid-whorl (SL/ PH = 2.2), weakly concave sides below apex, forming angle of about 55°. Rounded at strongly flaring base. Sculpture of close-set, relatively regular, indistinctly punctate, spiral grooves, delimiting broad, flat spiral cords. Spiral grooves occasionally somewhat irregular at intersections with growth lines. Punctuations not discernible as individual pits. Spiral cords even closer-set at apex. Aperture slightly longer than shell, moderately wide in adapical third (MD/AW = 5.8), widening greatly anteriorly (MD/MAW = 1.4), resulting in high expansion rate (ER = 4.1). Outer lip slightly alate and pointed apically, expanding and almost straight below, broadly rounded abapically. Basal lip solid, Columella deeply and broadly excavated. Columellar callus forming thick, broad, sharply delimited, adherent rim continuing into parietal callus of similar width and strength.

Discussion. Scaphander helveticus Peyrot, 1932 differs from Scaphander grundincola (De Gregorio, 1885) in its very broad, drop-shaped outline with faintly concave sides below the apex, more crowded spiral cords, and the thickened apical margin bordering the apical umbilicus. The type specimen from Salles (France) differs from the Paratethyan shell only in its slightly broader spiral cords. Specimens from the Atlantic Upper Miocene of Cacela illustrated by Dollfus et al. (1903: 22, pl. 36, figs 13–14) as Scaphander lignarius were discussed by Landau et al. (2013: 335). They are not that species, neither are they conspecific with any of the species discussed herein. They probably represent an undescribed species that will be discussed in a monograph describing the gastropod assemblage of the locality (Landau et al., in prep).

Paleoenvironment. Shallow marine, lagoonal (Harzhauser et al. 2002).

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Karpatian (Early Miocene): Korneuburg Basin: Niederkreuzstetten (Austria) (hoc opus).

Northeastern Atlantic. Serravallian (Middle Miocene): Aquitaine Basin: Salles, Sallespisse, Manciet (France) (Peyrot 1932).

metadata.title: Cosmophiline marinellii Berger 1949
metadata.description:
Cosmophiline marinellii Berger, 1949

Figs 54E 1 –E 3

* Cosmophiline marinellii n. gen. n. sp.— Berger 1949: 83.

Cosmophiline marinellii n. gen. n. sp.— Berger 1953: 115, pl. 18, fig. 87.

C [osmophiline]. marinellii Berg.— Sieber 1958: 164.

Type material. Holotype, NHMW-ZOO-MO-75000-E-4172, SL: 2.6 mm, MD: 1.4 mm, Bad Vöslau (Austria), Figs 54E 1 –E 3.

Revised description. Shell small, fragile, translucent, rectangular (SL/MD = 1.9). Spire covered by last whorl. Last whorl greatly expanded, wing like, with weak rounded angulation corresponding to shoulder and base. Sculpture above and below angulations of narrow, relatively strong spiral cords terminating as long pointed spines at periphery. Between angulations much weaker cords becoming subobsolete in broad mid-portion on last whorl (outer lip edge of mid-portion missing). Columella forming narrow rim in adapical part, thinning below. Aperture extremely wide, auriculate.

Discussion. This species is unique and is documented so far only by a single specimen.

Paleoenvironment. The occurrence in the Baden Formation at Bad Vöslau (Austria) suggests middle to outer neritic environments with up to 210 m water depth (Kranner et al. 2021).

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): Vienna Basin: Bad Vöslau (Austria) (Berger 1953).

metadata.title: Laona quadrata
metadata.description:
Laona quadrata (Wood, 1839)

* Bullaea quadrata Nob.— Wood 1839: 461, pl. 7, fig. 1.

Philine (Ossiania) quadrata (Wood) — Berger 1953: 114, pl. 18, fig. 81.

P [hiline]. (O [ssiania].) quadrata (Wood) — Sieber 1958: 164.

Laona quadrata (S.V. Wood, 1839) — Landau et al. 2023: 403, pl. 52, figs 1–2 [cum syn.].

Discussion. Berger (1953) illustrated a specimen from Bad Vöslau (Austria) (SL: 3.6, MD: 2.7) which is lost. The drawing corresponds well to shells of the Pliocene to Recent Laona quadrata as discussed by Landau et al. (2023). For description and discussion see Landau et al. (2023: 403).

Paleoenvironment. The occurrence in the Baden Formation suggests middle to outer neritic environments with up to 210 m water depth (Kranner et al. 2021).

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): Vienna Basin: Bad Vöslau (Austria) (Berger 1953).

Pliocene to present-day. North Sea, Atlantic, Mediterranean Sea (see Landau et al. (2023: 404 for details).

metadata.title: Roxania bomba Harzhauser, Landau & Malaquias, 2026, nov. nom.
metadata.description:
Roxania bomba nov. nom.

Figs 51D 1 –D 3

* Sabatia callifera helvetica n. subsp. — Berger 1949: 83.

Roxania (Sabatia) callifera helvetica n. subsp. — Berger 1953: 112, pl. 18, fig. 79 [non Roxania helvetica Peyrot, 1932].

R [oxania]. (S [abatia].) callifera helvetica Berg.— Sieber 1958: 164 [non Roxania helvetica Peyrot, 1932].

Type material. Holotype, NHMW-ZOO-MO-75000-E-18181A, SL: 4.9 mm, MD: 3.7 mm, Guntersdorf (Austria), Figs 51D 1 –D 3.

Etymology. Latin bomba (= bomb), referring to the shape; noun in apposition.

Revised description. Shell small, solid, very broadly ovate (SL/MD = 1.4), only last whorl visible. Last whorl strongly globose, apex rounded, narrow (MD/ApD = 3.0), deeply and narrowly umbilicate; low angled apical plane (AP = 5°). Sides of adapical part slightly conical; periphery convex, placed just above mid-whorl (SL/ PH = 1.9), rounded at base. Sculpture of punctate spiral grooves at extremities extending some distance towards mid-whorl, mid-portion smooth. Aperture slightly longer than shell, moderately narrow adapically (MD/AW = 7.1), strongly widening anteriorly (MD/MAW = 2.3), resulting in moderate expansion rate (ER = 3.1). Outer lip extending distinctly above apex adapically, rounded abapically. Columella short, thickened, forming broad, sharply delimited columellar rim bordering narrow umbilical chink. Parietal callus bearing small parietal pad.

Discussion. This species is difficult to characterize. Based on a single specimen, we have no information as to its intraspecific variability. Originally placed in Sabatia, it differs from members of that genus in its sculpture of punctate grooves at the extremities and its evenly and strongly rounded last whorl. Although it has a poorly defined parietal pad, it is not as strong or sharply delimited as in Sabatia species. Therefore, we follow Berger (1953) and place it in Roxania. This makes it a secondary homonym of Roxania helvetica Peyrot, 1932 from the Middle Miocene of France (Peyrot 1932: 197, pl. 13, figs 36, 40–42, 55). Therefore, we propose Roxania bomba nov. nom. as new name for this species.

Roxania bomba nov. nom. is most similar to Roxania callifera (Boettger, 1907) but differs in its weaker parietal pad and more strongly alate apex of the outer lip. It is possible that it is an extreme form of R. callifera, but until more material becomes available, we prefer to keep them separate. Sabatia isseli Bellardi, 1877, from the Mediterranean Pliocene, is strongly expanded abapically and bears a distinct ridge on the parietal callus rather than a poorly delimited pad (see Landau et al. 2023: pl. 57). The extant Sabatia pustulosa Dall, 1895, from New Caledonia, is also strongly inflated but has an even wider aperture and a very prominent, pustulose parietal callus (see Valdés 2008: fig. 48).

Paleoenvironment. Unknown.

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): North Alpine-Carpathian Foreland Basin: Grund, Guntersdorf (Austria) (Berger 1953).

metadata.title: Melanochlamys radwanskii
metadata.description:
Melanochlamys radwanskii (Bałuk, 2018)

Figs 50A 1 –A 2

* Chelidonura radwanskii sp. nov. — Bałuk 2018: 500, text-figs 2B1–2B2 (only) [non text-figs 2A1–2A2 = Chelidonura piserai sp. nov.].

Type material. Holotype, MWG UW ZI/93/1252, SL: 2.8 mm, MD: 2.2 mm, Badenian, Korytnica (Poland), illustrated in Bałuk (2018: text-fig. 2B), Figs 50A 1 –A 2.

Description. “ Protoconch very small, several microns in cross-section, with oval knob shape. Remaining part of shell composed of 1.5 strongly bent shield, straightening gradually towards front, becoming almost flat and much thinner. Also present wing increasing in size towards the posterior. Distinct thickening, in form of ridge running along posterior margin till end of wing, visible both on inner and outer side. Growth lines visible on external surface; internal surface smooth.” (Bałuk 2018: 500).

Discussion. Only two Aglajidae species have been described from the Paratethys prior to our revision: Chelidonura radwanskii Bałuk, 2018, from the Badenian of the Central Paratethys Sea, and Chelidonura jolkii (Anistratenko in Anistratenko & Prisyazhnyuk, 1996) from the Sarmatian of the Eastern Paratethys Sea (Ukraine). These species were placed by Guzhov (2023) in Chelidonura A. Adams, 1850, whereas Chaban & Martynov (2000) placed the Eastern Paratethyan species in Melanochlamys Cheeseman, 1881. For the Central Paratethys species Chelidonura radwanskii, we recognize that Bałuk (2018) illustrated two specimens representing two different genera. The holotype of Chelidonura radwanskii is a Melanochlamys, characterized by a very prominent crest along the wing, whereas the paratype of Chelidonura radwanskii is indeed that genus. Melanochlamys radwanskii is reminiscent of Melanochlamys cylindrica as discussed by Ortea et al. (2024) but differs in its even more prominent crest and weak concavity adjoining the ridge abapically. Chelidonura jolkii is exceptionally similar to Genus 3 of Ortea et al. (2024: fig. 6), which nests within the Chelidonura clade. Therefore, we follow Guzhov (2023) for the Eastern Paratethyan species.

Paleoenvironment. Melanochlamys species live on sandy-muddy bottoms on the littoral zone (Montety et al. 2024).

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): Korytnica Basin: Korytnica (Poland) (Bałuk 2018).

metadata.title: Melanochlamys Cheeseman 1881
metadata.description:
Genus Melanochlamys Cheeseman, 1881

Type species. Melanochlamys cylindrica Cheeseman, 1881, by monotypy. Present-day, New Zealand.

Discussion. Melanochlamys has a wide geographic distribution and occurs from temperate to tropical waters where it lives on soft bottoms prying on polychaetes and nemerteans cryptic diversity (Cooke et al. 2014)

metadata.title: Vellicolla postneglecta
metadata.description:
Vellicolla postneglecta (Berger, 1953)

Figs 49A–C

Retusa (Pyrunculus) postneglecta n. sp. — Berger 1949: 83 [nomen nudum].

* Retusa (Pyrunculus) postneglecta n. sp. — Berger 1953: 102, pl. 17, figs 49–51.

R [etusa]. (P [yrunculus].) postneglecta Berg.— Sieber 1958: 163.

Type material. Holotype, NHMW-ZOO-MO-75000-E-47055A, SL: 4.3 mm, MD: 2.1 mm, Lăpugiu (Romania), Figs 49A 1 –A 3. Paratypes, NHMW-ZOO-MO-75000-E-11797, SL: 4.2 mm, MD: 2.4 mm, Bad Vöslau (Austria), Figs 49B 1 –B 2; NHMW-ZOO-MO-75000-E-11797, SL: 4.2 mm, MD: 2.3 mm, Bad Vöslau (Austria), Figs 49C 1 –C 3.

Revised description. Shell small, medium-thickness, ovate-pyriform, broad (SL/MD = 1.8–2.0), only last whorl visible. Last whorl strongly inflated; periphery placed just below mid-whorl (SL/ PH = 2.2–2.5), rounded at rapidly contracting base. Apex produced, truncated, moderately narrow (MD/ApD = 2.5–2.8), deeply and narrowly umbilicate, apical plane strongly oblique (AP = 27–33°), delimited by round-edged elevated crest. Sculpture of a few spiral grooves just below apex weakening abapically, and over base, strengthening abapically. Aperture longer than shell, slightly widening at apex, moderately narrow below, narrowing towards mid-section (MD/AW = 7.3– 7.9), distinctly widening anteriorly (MD/MAW = 2.3–2.7), resulting in moderate low rate of 2.9–3.2. Outer lip narrowly alate apically, extending some distance above apex, weakly concave below. Columella short, with narrow columellar callus, delimiting narrow, deep umbilicus. No columellar fold. Parietal callus not developed.

Discussion. The sculpture of spiral grooves was overemphasized in the illustration of Berger (1953: pl. 17, fig. 49). For comparison with Vellicolla pannonica (Csepreghy-Meznerics, 1952), see above. Vellicolla postneglecta is reminiscent of the extant Pyrunculus pyriformis (Adams, 1850), from the Philippines, but differs in its less inflated last whorl and the wider umbilicus. Vellicolla okomotoi (Habe, 1952) is very similar but the latter has a higher placed periphery and the apical crest is more strongly constricted below. Pyrunculus cf. postneglectus of Stein et al. (2016: pl. 74, figs 4–5), from the early Miocene of the North Sea differs in its much broader apex and its outer lip is less raised above the apex.

Paleoenvironment. The occurrence in the Baden Formation at Bad Vöslau (Austria) suggests middle to outer neritic environments with up to 210 m water depth (Kranner et al. 2021).

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): Vienna Basin: Bad Vöslau (Austria) (Berger 1953); Făget Basin: Lăpugiu de Sus (Romania) (Berger 1953).
